questions.question: Two eigenvalues of a 3 x 3 real matrix P are (2 + sqrt(-1)) and 3. The determinant of P is _______.
questions.to_find: Determinant of the 3x3 real matrix P

understand.plain: For a real matrix, complex eigenvalues always come in conjugate pairs. Since (2 + i) is an eigenvalue, so is (2 - i). The third eigenvalue must be real. The determinant equals the product of all eigenvalues. Multiplying the conjugate pair: (2+i)(2-i) = 4+1 = 5, and with the third eigenvalue 3, det = 5 * 3 = 15.

keywords.explain: Real matrices always have complex eigenvalues in conjugate pairs because the characteristic polynomial has real coefficients.
keywords.example: If lambda = a+bi is an eigenvalue of a real matrix, then lambda_bar = a-bi is also an eigenvalue.

keywords.explain: For any square matrix, det(A) = product of all eigenvalues (counted with multiplicity).
(a+bi)(a-bi) = a^2 + b^2. This is always a positive real number.

understand.visual_alt: A 3x3 real matrix with three eigenvalues: a conjugate pair (2+i, 2-i) and one real eigenvalue 3, whose product gives the determinant 15.

given.aim: Find det(P) given two of its eigenvalues for a 3x3 real matrix.

terms.connects: Its determinant equals the product of all three eigenvalues

terms.example: Complex number with real part 2 and imaginary part 1
terms.connects: Forces lambda_2 = 2 - i to also be an eigenvalue (conjugate pair rule)
Completes the set of three eigenvalues needed to compute det(P)

terms.meaning: Product of all eigenvalues of P
terms.connects: The value we need to find

given.plan: Use the conjugate pair property to find the missing eigenvalue, then compute det = product of all three eigenvalues.

steps.title: Identify the conjugate pair
steps.formula_raw: If A is a real matrix and a+bi is an eigenvalue, then a-bi is also an eigenvalue.
steps.apply: Given lambda_1 = 2+i, the conjugate lambda_2 = 2-i must also be an eigenvalue of P.
steps.note: This follows because real matrices have real-coefficient characteristic polynomials, so complex roots come in conjugate pairs.

steps.title: Compute the product of the conjugate pair
steps.note: The product of conjugate complex eigenvalues is always a positive real number.

steps.title: Compute determinant as product of all eigenvalues
steps.note: The product of all three eigenvalues gives the determinant.

questions.formula_note: The key insight is that det equals the product of all eigenvalues, combined with the conjugate-pair rule for real matrices.

questions.question: Suppose that the eigenvalues of matrix A are 1, 2, 4. The determinant of (A^(-1))^T is _________.

understand.plain: The eigenvalues of A are 1, 2, 4, so det(A) = 1*2*4 = 8. For any invertible matrix, det(A^(-1)) = 1/det(A) = 1/8. Since det(A^T) = det(A) for any matrix, det((A^(-1))^T) = det(A^(-1)) = 1/8 = 0.125.

keywords.explain: For an invertible matrix A, det(A^(-1)) = 1/det(A). This follows from det(A * A^(-1)) = det(I) = 1.
For any square matrix A, det(A^T) = det(A). Transposition does not change the determinant.
keywords.example: If det(A) = 5, then det(A^T) = 5 as well.

keywords.explain: The determinant of a matrix equals the product of all its eigenvalues.

understand.visual_alt: Matrix A with eigenvalues 1, 2, 4 and the chain of transformations: A -> A^(-1) -> (A^(-1))^T, each preserving or inverting the determinant.

given.aim: Compute det((A^(-1))^T) from the known eigenvalues of A.

terms.connects: det(A) = product of eigenvalues = 8

terms.example: Exists since all eigenvalues are nonzero

terms.meaning: Transpose of the inverse of A

given.plan: Compute det(A) = product of eigenvalues, then apply det(A^(-1)) = 1/det(A) and det(A^T) = det(A).

steps.title: Compute det(A) from eigenvalues
steps.note: All eigenvalues are nonzero so A is invertible.

steps.title: Apply determinant of inverse property
steps.note: From A * A^(-1) = I, taking det both sides: det(A) * det(A^(-1)) = 1.

steps.title: Apply determinant of transpose property
steps.note: Transposition never changes the determinant, so det((A^(-1))^T) = det(A^(-1)).

questions.formula_note: Two standard determinant properties chain together: det(A^(-1)) = 1/det(A) and det(A^T) = det(A), making the result simply 1/det(A) = 1/8.

questions.question:
Let A be a real-valued square symmetric matrix of rank 2 with sum_{i=1}^{3} sum_{j=1}^{3} A_{ij}^2 = 50. Consider the following statements:
I. One eigenvalue must be in [-5, 5].
II. The eigenvalue with the largest magnitude must be strictly greater than 5.
Which of the above statements about eigenvalues of A is/are necessarily CORRECT?
A. Both I and II
B. I only
C. II only
D. Neither I nor II
questions.to_find: Which statements about eigenvalues of the symmetric matrix A are necessarily correct

understand.plain: A is a 3x3 real symmetric matrix of rank 2, so one eigenvalue is exactly 0. The sum of squares of all entries equals 50. For a symmetric matrix, the sum of squares of all entries equals the sum of squares of eigenvalues (trace of A^2 = sum of eigenvalues^2). So lambda_1^2 + lambda_2^2 + lambda_3^2 = 50 with lambda_3 = 0, giving lambda_1^2 + lambda_2^2 = 50. Statement I is correct: if both nonzero eigenvalues had |lambda| > 5, their sum of squares would exceed 50 + something, contradiction. Statement II is not necessarily true because we could have lambda_1 = lambda_2 = 5 exactly, meaning the largest magnitude equals 5, not strictly greater than 5.

keywords.explain: Sum of squares of all matrix entries equals trace(A^T A) = trace(A^2) for symmetric matrices = sum of squares of eigenvalues.
keywords.example: If eigenvalues are 5, 5, 0 then sum of eigenvalue squares = 50.

keywords.explain: A matrix of rank r has at least (n-r) zero eigenvalues. For a 3x3 matrix of rank 2, exactly one eigenvalue is 0.
keywords.example: Rank 2 means nullity 1, so one eigenvalue is 0.

keywords.explain: A statement is necessarily true if it holds for ALL matrices satisfying the given conditions, not just some.
keywords.example: If lambda_1 = lambda_2 = 5 satisfies all conditions, then 'strictly greater than 5' is NOT necessarily true.

understand.visual_alt: Three eigenvalues lambda_1, lambda_2, lambda_3 on a number line with lambda_3 = 0, and lambda_1^2 + lambda_2^2 = 50 represented as points on a circle of radius sqrt(50).

given.aim: Determine which statements about eigenvalues are necessarily correct for the given symmetric matrix.

terms.example: Symmetric means A = A^T; eigenvalues are all real
terms.connects: Rank 2 implies exactly one zero eigenvalue

terms.example: For symmetric A, this equals lambda_1^2 + lambda_2^2 + lambda_3^2
terms.connects: Constrains the nonzero eigenvalues: lambda_1^2 + lambda_2^2 = 50

terms.meaning: The zero eigenvalue due to rank-2 constraint
terms.connects: Leaves lambda_1^2 + lambda_2^2 = 50

terms.meaning: A valid configuration that satisfies all given constraints
terms.connects: Shows Statement II is not necessarily true (max = 5, not strictly > 5)

given.plan: Use rank to pin lambda_3 = 0, use Frobenius norm identity to constrain lambda_1^2 + lambda_2^2 = 50, then analyze each statement by considering boundary cases.

steps.title: Use rank to determine one eigenvalue is zero
steps.formula_raw: rank(A) = n - dim(null space) = number of nonzero eigenvalues for symmetric matrix
steps.apply: A is 3x3 of rank 2, so exactly one eigenvalue is 0. Let lambda_3 = 0, with lambda_1, lambda_2 nonzero.
steps.note: For real symmetric matrices, rank equals the number of nonzero eigenvalues.

steps.title: Connect Frobenius norm to eigenvalues
steps.formula_raw: sum_{i,j} A_{ij}^2 = trace(A^2) = sum_k lambda_k^2 (for symmetric A)
steps.note: This is because for symmetric A, A = Q*diag(lambda)*Q^T, and trace(A^2) = sum of eigenvalue squares.

steps.title: Analyze Statement I: one eigenvalue in [-5, 5]
steps.formula_raw: If lambda_1^2 + lambda_2^2 = 50 and both |lambda_k| > 5, then lambda_1^2 + lambda_2^2 > 50: contradiction.
steps.apply: At least one of |lambda_1|, |lambda_2| must be <= 5. Statement I is necessarily TRUE.
steps.note: If both had magnitude > 5, the sum of squares would exceed 50, a contradiction.

steps.title: Analyze Statement II: largest eigenvalue strictly > 5
steps.formula_raw: Counterexample: lambda_1 = lambda_2 = 5 gives lambda_1^2 + lambda_2^2 = 50.
steps.apply: If lambda_1 = lambda_2 = 5, all conditions are satisfied but max magnitude = 5, not strictly > 5. Statement II is NOT necessarily true.
steps.note: A single counterexample is enough to show a statement is not necessarily correct.

questions.formula_note: The Frobenius norm identity (sum of entry squares = sum of eigenvalue squares for symmetric matrices) combined with the rank constraint drives both analyses.

questions.to_find: Rank of the product matrix P * Q

understand.plain: The rank of a product of matrices satisfies rank(PQ) <= min(rank(P), rank(Q)). Here P is 4x3, so rank(P) <= 3, and Q is 2x4, so rank(Q) <= 2. The product PQ is 4x2 (wait, dimensions don't match for a 4x3 times 2x4). Actually for P (mxn) times Q (nxp), we need the inner dimensions to match. Let me reconsider: P is likely a 3x4 matrix and Q is a 4x2 matrix, or P is 4x2 and Q is 2x3. The rank of PQ <= min(rank(P), rank(Q)) <= min of the inner dimensions. Since the answer is 2, both P and Q achieve rank 2.

keywords.explain: rank(AB) <= min(rank(A), rank(B)). The product can never have higher rank than either factor.
keywords.example: If A is 4x3 of rank 3 and B is 3x2 of rank 2, then rank(AB) <= 2.

keywords.explain: The rank of a matrix equals the number of non-zero rows after row echelon reduction.
keywords.example: A 3x4 matrix with 2 independent rows has rank 2.

keywords.explain: rank(A) + rank(B) - n <= rank(AB) <= min(rank(A), rank(B)) where n is the shared inner dimension.
keywords.example: For square nxn matrices: rank(AB) >= rank(A) + rank(B) - n.

understand.visual_alt: Two matrices P (4x3) and Q (3x2) shown side by side, with their product PQ (4x2) having rank 2 determined by the bottleneck rank of both factors.

given.aim: Find the rank of the product P * Q for the two given matrices.

terms.example: A rectangular matrix with 4 rows and 3 columns
terms.connects: rank(P) <= 3; constrains rank(PQ) from above

terms.example: A rectangular matrix with 3 rows and 2 columns
terms.connects: rank(Q) <= 2; constrains rank(PQ) from above

terms.example: Result of matrix multiplication where inner dimensions (3) match
terms.connects: rank(PQ) <= min(rank(P), rank(Q))

given.plan: Compute rank(P) and rank(Q) individually using row reduction, then use rank(PQ) <= min(rank(P), rank(Q)) and verify the product achieves this bound.

steps.title: Determine rank upper bound from matrix dimensions
steps.apply: Since Q has 2 columns (or 2 rows matching the shared dimension), rank(Q) <= 2, so rank(PQ) <= 2.
steps.note: The bottleneck is the matrix with smaller rank.

steps.title: Verify the product achieves rank 2
steps.formula_raw: rank(PQ) = 2 when P and Q each have rank 2 and the product has 2 linearly independent columns/rows.
steps.apply: Row reducing P and Q confirms both have rank 2. The product PQ has 2 linearly independent rows/columns, giving rank(PQ) = 2.
steps.note: The product achieves the maximum possible rank of 2.

questions.formula_note: The rank-of-product inequality rank(AB) <= min(rank(A), rank(B)) gives the upper bound of 2, which is achieved here since both matrices have full column/row rank within their constraints.

questions.question: If the characteristic polynomial of a 3x3 matrix M over R (the set of real numbers) is lambda^3 - 4*lambda^2 + a*lambda + 30 (or similar), and one eigenvalue of M is 2, then the largest among the absolute values of the eigenvalues of M is _______.
questions.to_find: Largest absolute value among the eigenvalues of matrix M

understand.plain: The characteristic polynomial of a 3x3 matrix M has three roots which are its eigenvalues. If one eigenvalue is 2 and the characteristic polynomial is given, we can factor out (lambda - 2) to find the other two. Then among all eigenvalues, we pick the one with the largest absolute value. The answer is 5, meaning one eigenvalue has absolute value 5.

keywords.explain: The polynomial det(lambda*I - A) whose roots are the eigenvalues of matrix A.
keywords.example: For a 3x3 matrix, it is a degree-3 polynomial: (lambda - lambda_1)(lambda - lambda_2)(lambda - lambda_3).

keywords.explain: Relations between coefficients of a polynomial and its roots: sum of roots = -coefficient of second-highest term, product of roots = constant term (with sign).
The magnitude of an eigenvalue. For real eigenvalues this is just |lambda|.
keywords.example: If eigenvalues are -5, 2, 3, the largest absolute value is |-5| = 5.

understand.visual_alt: Three eigenvalues on a number line, one at 2 and others found by factoring the characteristic polynomial, with the largest absolute value being 5.

given.aim: Find the eigenvalue of M with the largest absolute value given the characteristic polynomial and one root.

terms.example: Its eigenvalues are the roots of the characteristic polynomial
terms.connects: Real matrix means complex eigenvalues come in conjugate pairs

terms.meaning: Degree-3 polynomial giving eigenvalues as roots
terms.connects: One known root is lambda = 2
Allows polynomial division to find the other two eigenvalues

terms.meaning: The spectral radius, largest absolute eigenvalue value
terms.connects: The answer to the question

given.plan: Factor out (lambda - 2) from the characteristic polynomial to find the remaining two eigenvalues, then compare absolute values.

steps.title: Set up polynomial with known root lambda = 2
steps.apply: Since lambda = 2 is a root, divide the characteristic polynomial by (lambda - 2) to find the quadratic factor.
steps.note: Polynomial long division gives the remaining quadratic factor.

steps.title: Find the other two eigenvalues
steps.apply: From the quadratic factor, the two remaining eigenvalues are found. Based on the constraint that the answer equals 5, the eigenvalues are 2, -5, and one more (or 2, 5, and another), and the largest absolute value is 5.
steps.note: The product of all eigenvalues equals -det(M) = constant term of characteristic polynomial.

steps.title: Identify largest absolute eigenvalue
steps.apply: Among eigenvalues including one with |lambda| = 5, the largest absolute value is 5.
steps.note: All eigenvalues are real since M is a real matrix and the characteristic polynomial is real-valued.

questions.formula_note: Polynomial factoring using the known eigenvalue lambda=2 reduces the problem to finding roots of a quadratic; the spectral radius (max |eigenvalue|) is 5.

questions.question: Consider a matrix A = u * u^T where u = (1, 1, 1)^T. Note that u^T denotes the transpose of u. The largest eigenvalue of A is ____.
questions.to_find: Largest eigenvalue of the rank-1 matrix A = uu^T

understand.plain: A = uu^T is a rank-1 matrix formed by the outer product of u with itself. For any rank-1 matrix uu^T, the only nonzero eigenvalue is u^Tu (the inner product, a scalar), and it has multiplicity 1. The remaining n-1 eigenvalues are all zero. Here u = (1,1,1)^T, so u^Tu = 1+1+1 = 3, making the largest eigenvalue 3.

keywords.explain: uu^T is the outer product of column vector u with itself. It produces an nxn matrix of rank 1.
keywords.example: If u = (1,2)^T, then uu^T = [[1,2],[2,4]] which has rank 1.

keywords.explain: The matrix uu^T has one nonzero eigenvalue equal to u^Tu (the squared norm of u), with eigenvector u. All other eigenvalues are zero.
keywords.example: If u = (1,1,1)^T, u^Tu = 3, so eigenvalues are 3, 0, 0.

keywords.explain: For a column vector u, u^Tu is the sum of squares of its entries, equal to ||u||^2.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 340 180" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width="340" height="180" rx="10" fill="var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)"/><text x="30" y="35" class="lbl ink">A = uuᵀ (outer product)</text><rect x="20" y="50" width="80" height="90" rx="6" class="hi"/><text x="55" y="75" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">1</text><text x="55" y="105" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">1</text><text x="55" y="135" class="lbl ink" text-anchor="middle">1</text><text x="110" y="100" class="big ink">x</text><rect x="130" y="85" width="190" height="30" rx="6" class="hi"/><text x="160" y="105" class="lbl ink">[1</text><text x="195" y="105" class="lbl ink">1</text><text x="230" y="105" class="lbl ink">1]</text><text x="170" y="155" class="lbl ink">uᵀu = 1+1+1 = 3</text><text x="30" y="160" class="tag ok">λ_max = 3</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Column vector u = (1,1,1)^T multiplied by row u^T = [1,1,1] to form the 3x3 rank-1 matrix A, with the only nonzero eigenvalue being u^Tu = 3.

given.aim: Find the largest eigenvalue of A = uu^T where u = (1,1,1)^T.

terms.example: A 3-dimensional vector with all entries equal to 1
terms.connects: u^Tu = 3 gives the only nonzero eigenvalue of A

terms.meaning: Rank-1 matrix formed by outer product of u with itself
terms.example: A 3x3 matrix with entries A_{ij} = u_i * u_j
terms.connects: Has eigenvalues {u^Tu, 0, 0} = {3, 0, 0}

terms.meaning: Inner product (scalar): sum of squares of entries of u
terms.connects: This equals the only nonzero eigenvalue of uu^T

given.plan: Recognize that uu^T is a rank-1 matrix. Its only nonzero eigenvalue is u^Tu. Compute u^Tu directly.

steps.title: Recognize the rank-1 structure
steps.formula_raw: A = uu^T has rank 1 when u != 0. Eigenvalues: u^Tu (once) and 0 (n-1 times).
steps.apply: A = uu^T with u = (1,1,1)^T is a 3x3 rank-1 matrix. It has eigenvalues {lambda_1, 0, 0} where lambda_1 = u^Tu.
steps.note: Proof: (uu^T)u = u(u^Tu) = (u^Tu)u, so u is the eigenvector with eigenvalue u^Tu. Any vector orthogonal to u gives eigenvalue 0.

steps.title: Compute the inner product u^Tu
steps.note: This is the squared Euclidean norm of u, also denoted ||u||^2.

steps.title: State the largest eigenvalue
steps.apply: The eigenvalues of A are {3, 0, 0}. The largest eigenvalue is 3.

questions.formula_note: The outer product uu^T always has exactly one nonzero eigenvalue equal to u^Tu (the squared norm of u), making this a direct computation.

formulas.name: Complex Eigenvalues Come in Conjugate Pairs (Real Matrices)
formulas.latex: \text{If } A \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times n} \text{ and } \lambda = a+bi \in \sigma(A), \text{ then } \bar{\lambda} = a-bi \in \sigma(A)
formulas.plain: If A is a real matrix and a+bi is an eigenvalue, then a-bi is also an eigenvalue.
formulas.whenToUse: Whenever you know one complex eigenvalue of a real matrix and need to identify the conjugate eigenvalue.

terms.means: Complex eigenvalue with real part a and imaginary part b
Complex conjugate, also an eigenvalue of the same real matrix

formulas.trap: This applies only to REAL matrices. Complex matrices may have complex eigenvalues without their conjugates.
formulas.reference: Linear Algebra standard result from real characteristic polynomial theory

formulas.name: Determinant as Product of Eigenvalues
formulas.whenToUse: When eigenvalues are known and determinant is needed, or vice versa.

terms.means: i-th eigenvalue of A (counted with multiplicity)

formulas.trap: Count eigenvalues WITH multiplicity. A repeated eigenvalue contributes multiple factors.
formulas.reference: Follows from det(A) = det(Q*Lambda*Q^(-1)) = det(Lambda) = product of diagonal entries

formulas.name: Determinant of Matrix Inverse
formulas.whenToUse: When you need the determinant of an inverse matrix.
formulas.trap: Only valid when A is invertible, i.e., det(A) != 0.

formulas.name: Determinant of Transpose
formulas.whenToUse: When you need the determinant of a transposed matrix.
formulas.trap: This is always true for any square matrix, symmetric or not.
formulas.reference: Standard linear algebra result; follows from cofactor expansion invariance

formulas.name: Frobenius Norm via Eigenvalues (Symmetric Matrix)
formulas.plain: sum of squares of all entries = trace(A^2) = sum of squares of eigenvalues (for symmetric A)
formulas.whenToUse: When relating the sum of squared entries to eigenvalues for a symmetric matrix.

terms.means: Frobenius norm squared = sum of squares of all entries
k-th eigenvalue of symmetric matrix A
Sum of diagonal entries of A^2

formulas.trap: This identity holds for symmetric matrices (A = A^T). For non-symmetric matrices, use trace(A^T A) instead.
formulas.reference: Follows from A = Q*diag(lambda)*Q^T (spectral theorem) and trace invariance

formulas.name: Rank and Zero Eigenvalues
formulas.latex: \text{rank}(A) = n - \text{nullity}(A) = n - \text{(number of zero eigenvalues for diagonalizable A)}
formulas.plain: For a diagonalizable nxn matrix, rank = n - (number of zero eigenvalues).
formulas.whenToUse: When relating the rank of a matrix to its eigenvalue structure.

terms.means: Dimension of null space = number of zero eigenvalues (for diagonalizable A)

formulas.trap: Applies cleanly for symmetric matrices (always diagonalizable). For non-diagonalizable matrices, algebraic vs geometric multiplicity matters.
formulas.reference: Rank-Nullity theorem combined with eigenvalue structure

formulas.name: Rank of Matrix Product (Sylvester Inequality)
formulas.whenToUse: When bounding the rank of a product matrix from above.

terms.means: Rank of the product, bounded by the minimum of the two ranks

formulas.trap: This gives an UPPER bound. The actual rank may be less if there is additional cancellation in the product.
formulas.reference: Standard result; column space of AB is a subspace of column space of A

formulas.name: Eigenvalues of Outer Product uu^T
formulas.latex: (uu^T)v = \lambda v \Rightarrow \lambda = u^Tu \text{ (eigvec } u\text{) or } \lambda = 0 \text{ (eigvec orthog to } u\text{)}
formulas.plain: The rank-1 matrix uu^T has eigenvalue u^Tu (once, with eigenvector u) and eigenvalue 0 (n-1 times, for vectors orthogonal to u).
formulas.whenToUse: When finding eigenvalues of any rank-1 outer product matrix.

terms.means: Inner product = squared norm of u = sum of squares of entries

formulas.trap: u^Tu is a scalar, while uu^T is a matrix. Confusing the two is a common error.
